title: What is Ownership? 👑
description: The most unique and important feature of Rust.

slides.title: The Big Problem 💣
slides.content:
All programs have to manage the computer's memory while running.

* **C/C++**: You must manually allocate and free memory. Miss one, and your program crashes instantly or gets hacked.
* **Python/Java/JS**: A background system called a 'Garbage Collector' constantly scans and deletes unused memory, which slows down your app unpredictably.

slides.title: The Rust Solution 🦀
slides.content:
Rust takes a revolutionary third approach: **Ownership**.

Memory is managed through a system of strict rules that the compiler checks *before the program even runs*. 

If you break an ownership rule, your code simply will not compile. Period.

quizData.question: How does Rust manage memory?

options.text: It requires you to manually call 'free(memory)'
A runtime garbage collector periodically cleans it
Ownership rules enforced entirely at compile-time

quizData.explanation: Ownership ensures memory safety instantly at compile-time without slowing down runtime performance.

slides.title: The Three Rules of Ownership 📜
slides.content:
You must memorize these three absolute rules of Rust:

1. Each value in Rust has an **owner** (a variable).
2. There can only be **one** owner at a time.
3. When the owner goes out of scope, the value will be **dropped** (deleted from memory).

slides.title: Rule 3 in Action 🗑️

quizData.question: What happens when the current *owner* variable goes out of scope?

options.text: It waits for the Garbage Collector

quizData.explanation: Once the scope ends (at the curly brace `}`), the variable dies and Rust automatically removes all its data from RAM to prevent leaks.
